data.title: GSI Technology: A Speculative Buy Scaling To Profitability
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I’ve held off from covering too many AI stocks because there is so much news coming out that feels almost daily that shifts the market sentiment. It’s a real roller coaster. Today is an exception, though, with GSI Technology (GSIT) which is starting to develop what could be a revolutionary technology that cuts down on energy usage and eliminates the need for GPUs and CPUs in certain environments.

It’s a speculative buy, but I think not having exposure would leave a lot of potential on the table. The balance sheet is healthy, there is a clear path towards profitability through scaling, and if I’m proven wrong in my forecast, there is a lot of further upside. I’m rating the stock a Buy.

Business Model - In The Scaling Stage

In their financial reporting GSIT describes itself as a:

We are a provider of high-performance semiconductor memory solutions for in-place associative computing applications in high growth markets such as artificial intelligence and high-performance computing, including natural language processing and computer vision.

This covers the legacy business of GSIT which consists of SRAM memory chips. Most people know about RAM and even DRAM as well. The main difference here with SRAM is that the memory chip sits inside the networking equipment, meaning that the data processing is done “in-house,” so to speak. It often means higher costs, but the trade off is that it’s far more reliable and has a faster response. This is all because it eliminates the need for constant refreshes that RAM and DRAM require.

GSIT is a fabless company, meaning it only designs the chips and then outsources the work to TSMC (TSM), for example, to produce the chips. Right now the only revenues that GSIT generates are from this legacy business. But 'legacy' doesn't quite do it justice, seeing as the revenues grew by 38% YoY based on the first 6 months of 2025. 41% if based on the Q2 figures.

Reporter

Forecasted CAGR

Expected Market Size

Mordor Intelligence

5.6% Until 2030

$2.25 billion in 2030

Growth Market Reports

27.3% Until 2033

$10.36 billion

Claight Corporation

3.5% Until 2035

$891 million in 2035

Data Bridge Research

5% Until 2030

$1 billion in 2030

Industry reports are mixed on what the growth for SRAM will be over the next several years. Some are very bullish and actually mimic the same momentum that GSIT has posted recently.

Over the past 6 years, revenues have fallen sharply for GSIT, nearly 50% from 2019 levels. With this, the operating losses have also widened to $5.3 million during the first 6 months of 2025. But, through this, the gross margins have actually held up very well. This suggests the success of GSIT is entirely tied to its ability to scale its sales pipelines more than anything else. Operating expenses are actually down since 2019, from $16.963 million to $12.547 million (6M basis).

In October of 2025, the company raised $50 million in equity. The annualized operating loss is $10.726 million, which suggests this helped provide a runway of 4.66 more years of operations. Part of the reason why I’m not that concerned with GSIT not yet producing a profit is because its balance sheet, for instance, is incredibly healthy with no traditional debt. On top of this, 21% of the shares are held by insiders, and around 15% by institutions.

Associative Process Unit - APU, The New Growth Opportunity

Having covered the legacy business, we can move on to the very reason GSIT's stock price has risen by over 160% the past 12 months. You see, back in October 2025, a very favorable report came out that analyzed the current GEMINI-1 APU unit that GSIT actually has in production.

The segment aims to solve one of the most important issues that AI companies are facing. Energy costs. When there is a massive amount of data that needs to be moved back and forth between GPUs and CPUs the energy costs are immense. The APU solves this by doing the data processing in-memory or “compute-in-memory” instead. This eliminates the need for separate memories and makes a request that could take minutes to go down to microseconds.

The main target markets that GSIT has are edge AI. This includes drones, military vehicles, or robotics. An APU offers an advantage where it eliminates the need for a GPU or a CPU. Take a drone, for example; not needing these components both brings down the production cost and the performance as well. Military vehicles too. When speed can be the difference between life and death, having the fastest is essential. With President Trump also pushing to increase the US military budget by over 50% to $1.5 trillion, means capital could be flooding into this space.

We shouldn't be confusing GSIT with being a competitor to NVIDIA (NVDA) or Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) in the data center space. It’s too competitive and GSIT would have no chance of disrupting right now no matter how good its technology might be. I say this because there are so many orders already for NVIDIA’s chips, GSIT doesn't have the contracts with TSMC or the capacity to make a meaningful difference here. It makes more sense to focus on edge applications like drones and military vehicles where growing market share is likely easier. As time goes on I can see these APUs being used in data centers, but that’s not what the current investment thesis I have is based on.

with a fully optimized version available in the first half of calendar 2026. This algorithm would be for defense applications such as drones, satellites and other military vehicles.

In the most recent earnings call GSIT outlined the roadmap to production for the APU segment, citing H1 2026 to include the first shipments.

We are embarking on the journey towards a major milestone, the tape-out of Plato chip in early calendar 2027.

The Plato chip is the third-generation APU chip which is aimed specifically at the large-language models. The use case mostly includes embedded AI applications in smartphones or autonomous vehicles. It marks a small shift for GSIT as it broadened its end markets from military and drones to other industries.

There are no mentions of what the revenues will look like here, and management is being vague, I think, because it’s still pre-shipment. But based on the $6.8 billion earlier stated TAM figure in 2025, if GSIT just gets 0.5% of this share, it could translate to $34 million in annual revenues. This would be transformative for the business and justify the jump the share price has done.

The Cornell Study - A Potential Gamechanger

In October of 2025 Cornell University found that the technology that GSIT has in its APU SRAM could be a game changer in the world of AI. Its performance is faster, and its energy usage is far lower. These are the key metrics that customers value. If data centers can push down energy costs, which are estimated to be the largest expense line, with some estimates at 46% of all costs. The second line being speed. As discussed earlier, what differentiates GSIT is that the processing is done in memory, which eliminates the need to move data back and forth. This adds to the energy costs of course but also means AI companies like OpenAI or Grok can train their models both faster and cheaper, potentially being the missing link that investors are looking for as to how these ventures could eventually turn a profit. But think of this as more of a long, long-term opportunity, not a near-term one. NVDA and AMD are too dominant in this space for GSIT to enter and make a difference.

One of the key findings in the Cornell study was the latency breakdown for RAG retrievals. This table shows the time spent searching a massive database to find the return statement. The bottleneck is the calculation distance. It shows that without optimization the time for a 200GB database was 527.9 ms, but with optimization it fell to 74.6 ms, being 7x faster.

This directly proves that the tech GSIT has works and is faster than what is currently readily available on the market right now. Compute heavy searches are now something far more manageable.

The second important figure was the energy usage. For a 200GB database the energy usage was 686.7 joules for a GPU, whilst for a APU it was only 12.6 joules That is only 1.8% of the energy usage of GPUs. It completely revolutionizes the cost profile for data centers if- and it's a big if—GSIT is able to expand into this market in the future.

Pre-Profit, But Getting There Quickly Through Scaling

Going back to the current business of GSIT. It guided for $6 - $6.8 million in revenues for the third quarter as it advanced efforts with GEMINI-II. Annualized, this implies around $25.6 million in net revenues. With a market cap of $276 million, it means GSIT trades at 10.7x sales. I think a 25% top line growth rate is well within reason if GSIT starts shipping out in H1 2026, and more in H2.

Based on this table, I see GSIT reaching $10.4 million in net income by 2030 if it can continue to scale and the APUs are well-received on the market. This values shares at 26.5x 2030 earnings. It’s still a buy for me because of the upside that still might not be baked into the model here. With the way the AI industry has been moving so far there seems to be no lack of capital to throw at new technology. If companies start getting desperate to generate a profit they might turn to GSIT in larger masses. Because of it being fabless it also means this demand can be translated if TSMC has the capacity or opens up more.

There is a clear path towards profitability I think as it’s all tied to increasing the sales pipelines and this is exactly what the APUs will be doing for the company. I’m a buyer of course, but I’m not selling the house and getting more shares here. Having a smaller percentage of exposure seems like the best way forward.

Risks

The main risks I’ve identified with GSIT are clearly outlined in the SEC Filings. GSIT mentions how it has issues raising the average selling price per unit, the unit in this case being the SRAM memory chips of course. Because of this GSIT is inadvertently more impacted by downturns in the semiconductor industry. There is a level of cyclicality which I think is tied to access to cheap capital. Periods where interest rates are lower often seem to coincide with periods of more demand in the industry.

The average selling price of our products has increased or remained unchanged in recent years. However, as is typical in the semiconductor industry, the selling prices of our products has historically declined over the life of the product.

The risk this presents is that GSIT will see far more aggressive swings in net revenues. It can’t pass on some of this by raising prices because then it would most likely lose out on market share instead.

can also be affected by our ability to increase production through the availability of increased wafer fabrication capacity from TSMC, our wafer supplier

The second risk is a consequence of the business model GSIT has. Being a fabless business it means that sales are entirely dependent on the capacity it has with TSMC. The company is under immense pressure to increase capacity as it’s one of just a few factories in the world able to supply. Back in November the company mentioned how even after investments it’s still falling about 3x short of the advanced-node demand, tied to AI demand. I would make the argument that the success of GSIT is in large part tied to the success and access to capacity of TSMC. This means one of the main risks of GSIT is out of its control. To some investors this might be the final straw. Personally, I’m still comfortable buying because of the upside in the developing APU segment.

The Full Picture

The market has clearly taken notice to GSIT with how the stock moved after the Cornell report last October. It’s now up to management to ensure that the shipment for GEMINI-II starts in H1 2026 and is able to scale further. Ironically the APUs might solve a bottleneck with data processing, GSIT itself faces a potential bottleneck with TSMC if capacity isn't available at the required scale. This might be a short-term setback, but not something that puts me off from opening a position in the stock in correlation with this article. I'm rating shares a Buy.

data.title: Franklin Equity Income Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: US equities delivered strong performance over the third quarter of 2025, buoyed, in part, by easing trade tensions and optimism surrounding artificial intelligence (AI). The US Federal Reserve’s (Fed’s) interest-rate cut in September also supported market sentiment, as did generally robust corporate earnings. Both the S&P 500 Index and the Nasdaq Composite Index ended the quarter at new record highs, while returns for the Dow Jones Industrial Average were more muted. In this environment, growth investing surpassed value in the large-capitalization tier, while it was the reverse in the mid- and small-capitalization segments, with small-cap stocks overall performing better than their large- and mid-cap counterparts.
  • Contributors: The fund outperformed (Advisor Class without sales charges) the Russell 1000 Value Index as stock selection decisions worked in our favor. In absolute terms, the financials, industrials, health care and information technology (IT) sectors had the largest positive impact on returns.
  • Detractors: Allocation decisions detracted from results relative to the benchmark. In absolute terms, materials was the only sector that turned negative due to our chemicals industry exposure.
  • Outlook: As markets have fully recovered from April lows and many indexes have reached new highs, we are mindful of valuation risks and higher expectations that are present. Our focus remains on the opportunities for individual companies held in the fund as well as on understanding the durability of end markets and the earnings impact of tariffs.

Morningstar Rating™

Overall Rating as of September 30, 2025

★★★★

(4-Star) Advisor Class

★★★★

(4-Star) Class A

As of 09/30/2025 the fund’s Advisor Class and Class A shares received a 4 star overall Morningstar rating™, measuring risk- adjusted returns against 1086, 1025 and 842 Large Value funds over the 3-, 5- and 10- year periods, respectively. A fund’s overall rating is derived from a weighted average of the performance 1gures associated with its 3-, 5- and 10-year (if applicable) rating metrics.

Top Holdings (% of Total)

Performance Review

  • Morgan Stanley (MS) delivered strong performance in the financial sector. The global financial services company reported solid second-quarter 2025 financial results driven by growth in its institutional and wealth management businesses, while an increase in capital markets improved its forward outlook.
  • United Rentals (URI) was the strongest performer within the industrials sector. The company is seeing improved sequential trends, including growth in fleet productivity, better-than-expected general rent revenue and sequential progress on gross profit margins. In contrast, heating, ventilation and air conditioning company Carrier Global detracted from sector returns despite issuing solid second-quarter financial results.
  • The health care sector benefited from a position in Johnson & Johnson (JNJ). The pharmaceutical giant reported strong second-quarter financial results and raised its full-year guidance, bolstered by the addition of Intra-Cellular Therapies (an acquisition completed in April 2025) to its neuroscience portfolio. We continue to see JNJ as a company with a highly resilient business and a leader in the medical technology industry
  • Conversely, in the consumer staples sector, Philip Morris International’s (PM) shares declined over the period due to a mix of profit-taking by some investors, weakening cigarette volumes and a slight second-quarter 2025 revenue miss despite the company exceeding consensus earnings estimates and raising its full-year guidance. The company’s tobacco-free products, however, continue to grow strongly.
  • Elsewhere, a position in Texas Instruments hindered returns in the IT sector. The chipmaker’s stock fell because of disappointing third-quarter guidance, signaling a slow recovery in key markets.

Outlook

  • We continue to look for opportunities where we expect realized earnings to be either more resilient or grow faster than consensus and where long-term market leadership is underappreciated. Today’s environment presents some uncertainty; however, we believe themes around IT productivity, broad infrastructure, and asset management could prove to be particularly attractive and durable.
  • We have had a positive view of the outlook for corporate earnings and financial markets overall, given the likelihood of lower regulations aiding several industries, favorable tax policy and ongoing productivity gains from technology. We believe this could support broader market participation across industries.
  • Despite the positive backdrop that we see, some companies may report mixed results in the third quarter due to uneven order patterns and tariff headwinds. However, we believe the transitory impacts of tariffs may be more muted, particularly among strong companies benefiting from secular themes. If correct, we believe our efforts to find these high-quality companies can have beneficial outcomes over time.
  • Equity-linked notes and convertible securities continue to be helpful tools for performance, risk mitigation and income generation. Given our focus on quality and the use of these securities, we believe the portfolio is positioned to navigate periods of higher volatility.

Performance data quoted represents past performance, which does not guarantee future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the figures shown. Principal value and investment returns will fluctuate, and investors’ shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than the original cost. Performance would have been lower if fees had not been waived in various periods. Total returns assume the reinvestment of all distributions and the deduction of all fund expenses. Returns with sales charge reflect a deduction of the stated maximum sales charge. An investor cannot invest directly in an index, and unmanaged index returns do not reflect any fees, expenses or sales charges. Returns for periods of less than one year are not annualized. All classes of shares may not be available to all investors or through all distribution channels. For current month-end performance, please visit franklintempleton.com.

The fund began offering Advisor Class shares on 5/15/2008. Performance quotations have been calculated as follows: (a) for Advisor Class periods prior to 5/15/2008, a restated figure is used based on the fund’s Class A performance. The performance was adjusted to take into account differences in class-specific operating expenses and maximum sales charges. (b) For periods after share class offering, performance for the specific share class is used, reflecting the expenses and maximum sales charges applicable to that class.

Gross expenses are the fund’s total annual operating expenses as of the fund’s prospectus available at the time of publication. Actual expenses may be higher and may impact portfolio returns. Net expenses reflect contractual fee waivers, expense caps and/or reimbursements, which cannot be terminated prior to 02/28/2026 without Board consent. Additional amounts may be voluntarily waived and/or reimbursed and may be modified or discontinued at any time without notice.

What are the Risks?

All investments involve risks, including possible loss of principal. Equity securities are subject to price fluctuation and possible loss of principal. Distributions are not guaranteed and are subject to change. Investments in equity-linked notes often have risks similar to their underlying securities, which could include management risk, market risk and, as applicable, foreign securities and currency risks. Convertible securities are subject to the risks of stocks when the underlying stock price is high relative to the conversion price and debt securities when the underlying stock price is low relative to the conversion price. International investments are subject to special risks, including currency fluctuations and social, economic and political uncertainties, which could increase volatility. These risks are magnified in emerging markets. The manager may consider environmental, social and governance (ESG) criteria in the research or investment process; however, ESG considerations may not be a determinative factor in security selection. In addition, the manager may not assess every investment for ESG criteria, and not every ESG factor may be identified or evaluated. These and other risks are discussed in the fund’s prospectus.

Glossary

Convertible securities are bonds or preferred stocks that can be converted into a preset number of shares of common stocks after a predetermined date.

Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA) is an unmanaged index composed of 30 blue-chip stocks, each with annual sales exceeding $7 billion. The DJIA is price-weighted, reflects large-cap companies representative of U.S. industry, and historically has moved in tandem with other major market indexes such as the S&P 500. Source: © S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC.

Nasdaq Composite Index is a market-capitalization-weighted index that is designed to represent the performance of NASDAQ securities and includes over 3,000 stocks. Source: Nasdaq OMX.

Important Information

The information provided is not a complete analysis of every material fact regarding any country, market, industry, security or fund. Because market and economic conditions are subject to change, comments, opinions and analyses are rendered as of the date of this material and may change without notice. A portfolio manager’s assessment of a particular security, investment or strategy is not intended as individual investment advice or a recommendation or solicitation to buy, sell or hold any security or to adopt any investment strategy; it is intended only to provide insight into the fund’s portfolio selection process. Holdings are subject to change.

The Russell 1000 Value Index measures the performance of the large-cap value segment of the U.S. equity universe. Source: FTSE. The S&P 500 Index features 500 leading U.S. publicly traded companies, with a primary emphasis on market capitalization. Source: © S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC. All rights reserved.

Important data provider notices and terms available at www.franklintempletondatasources.com. All data is subject to change.

Morningstar Rating™: Source: Morningstar®, 09/30/2025. For each mutual fund and ETF with at least a 3-year history, Morningstar calculates a Morningstar Rating™ based on how a fund ranks on a Morningstar Risk-Adjusted Return measure against other funds in the same category. This measure takes into account variations in a fund’s monthly performance, and does not take into account the effects of sales charges and loads, placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. The top 10% of funds in each category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars and the bottom 10% receive 1 star. The weights are: 100% 3-year rating for 36-59 months of total returns, 60% 5-year rating/40% 3-year rating for 60-119 months of total returns, and 50% 10-year rating/30% 5-year rating/20% 3-year rating for 120 or more months of total returns. While the 10-year overall star rating formula seems to give the most weight to the 10-year period, the most recent 3-year period actually has the greatest impact because it is included in all three rating periods. The fund’s Class A shares received a Morningstar Rating of 4, 3 and 4 and fund’s Advisor Class shares received a Morningstar Rating of 4, 3 and 4 star(s) for the 3-, 5- and 10-year periods, respectively. Franklin Templeton provides this fund’s Morningstar Rating™ for Class A and Advisor Class shares only. Other share classes may have different Morningstar ratings.

Franklin Distributors, LLC. Member FINRA/SIPC.

data.title: Where To Get Highest Treasury Yields, It Is Not Via GOVZ
data.description:
We have read the tea leaves on iShares 25+ Year Treasury STRIPS Bond ETF (GOVZ) fairly well. Our Strong Sell rating worked the best in 2023, with the ETF delivering negative 25% returns and negative 18% total returns since.

We obviously have warmed up to the bonds since then, as the diminishing stern tone below demonstrates.

We review the latest developments and tell you where investors can actually get higher yields than what GOVZ offers. But first, a quick recap.

The STRIPS Fund

GOVZ owns STRIPS, a form of Treasury bond that pays no interest, but gets you all of that compounded in the form of a large maturity payment. GOVZ focuses on the longest duration of this subset. So these bonds are sold at huge discounts to par value and at first glance look like bonds of a CCC- rated emerging market country. But that price makes perfect sense as you are foregoing years and years of interest payments. With GOVZ, though, you do get distributions every quarter. This might sound weird, but it is largely done to pass through the "taxable income". Even with STRIPS, the IRS is always standing hat in hand for your money. So while payment is made on these a long time from now, there is an implied interest calculated every year as the bond appreciates closer to par. This is the taxable income. GOVZ pays this out to you by selling some bonds.

As you go across the spectrum of Treasuries, from bills to shorter-term bonds and all the way out to 30-year bonds, you will find GOVZ offers the highest yield you can find. iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT) pales in comparison with just 4.36% trailing versus 4.93% from GOVZ. The key reason is that GOVZ has a lot more duration risk, and STRIPS are the most sensitive to movements in interest rates. You can see that when you compare their performance from late December 2021 to date. We have thrown in iShares 7-10 Year Treasury as well for another comparative.

But because of its expense structure, GOVZ remains the best thing to own if you really want a long-dated Treasury ETF. Of course, we are here to tell you that you can do better.

The Alternative

Before we get to that, let's get some exact numbers so that we can make accurate comparisons. The trailing yield shown previously is fine for ballpark, but here we want to get into a little more depth. The current yield is about 4.87% for the fund. 4.97% yield to maturity minus the expenses.

This is based on the January 9th close, but GOVZ is within 1 cent (lower) of that closing price. So we can assume that the current yield is about 4.88% or so. Great. So what's the alternative?

You can buy your own STRIP bonds, but you have to do this intelligently. Below, we show how not to do it.

The February 2055, which is about 30 years out and has a yield to maturity around 4.94% (midpoint of bid of 4.96% and ask of 4.921%).

This is fairly close to GOVZ, and if you extract the 10 basis points of expense, this is what you are getting with GOVZ. But the US Treasury curve features a hump. This is a rather unique setup where Treasuries between 20 and 30-year timeframes actually yield higher than the 30-year. This phenomenon has existed for a long time, and we don't bore you with the countless explanations people have made to explain it. But it does exist. Of course, to make it easier for you, we are not going to get you to even search through that. We will just bring up the highest yield for you on a platter. It is the August 15, 2050, STRIP bond shown below.

This is about 5.09% at the midpoint and gives you 15 basis points higher and 5 years earlier maturity (hence slightly lower duration risk) than the longest maturity. Combine that with the expense ratio saved, and you've got yourself 25 basis points of free alpha just for the taking.

Outlook & Verdict

We think GOVZ is a far more attractive proposition today than it was in 2023. We think the risks for a deflationary wave are higher than those of an inflationary wave at this point. At the minimum, investors benchmarking on 60:40 indices need to have the 40% allocation in there, at least in our view. We had been bearish during the 2020-2023 timeframe and neutral in the 2024-2025 timeframe. At this point, you could color us bullish. We have gone quite heavy ourselves on the allocations and are now trending near 65%. So we can get behind GOVZ at this point from an investing stance. But we are still not going to put a buy on it simply because we want to emphasize that investors should still choose the highest yields available for them. We also want to emphasize that it is very easy to find alpha-producing opportunities in bonds, whether they be Treasury bonds or corporate bonds. We rate GOVZ a Hold.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

data.title: Delta Air Lines: $7 EPS Ceiling
data.description:
Delta Air Lines, Inc. (DAL) just reported a solid quarter and guided to decent growth in 2026, but the airline appears stuck in Neutral. The stock has traded up to all-time highs around $70, so the market probably wants to see progress on the airline moving past a constant $7 EPS target. My investment thesis is Bullish on Delta Air Lines due to the valuation and opportunity.

Q4 Earnings

Delta Air Lines generally reported solid Q4'25 earnings with a revenue beat as follows:

The company followed up those numbers with the following metrics and guidance:

  • Record revenue in the December quarter and full year with a double-digit return on invested capital.
  • Accelerating momentum with 2026 earnings expected to grow 20 percent year-over-year.
  • Strong fundamentals support the March quarter revenue growth outlook of 5 to 7 percent over the prior year.

On the surface, the numbers sound huge and reflective of why the stock is trading at all-time highs. Below the surface, Delta Air Lines only reported a Q4 EPS of $1.55, down from the $1.85 reported last Q4, despite the revenue growth in 2025.

The airline sector faces constant cost pressures, including Delta reporting a 4% increase in non-fuel CASM in Q4. The airline sector faces constant labor pressure, making earnings growth difficult, even if the business is already solidly profitable.

Another $7 EPS Forecast

Delta Air Lines guided to 2026 EPS of $6.50 to $7.50, up an impressive 20% from the $5.82 just reported for last year. The issue here is that the airline originally provided a 2025 guidance of at least a $7.35 EPS and initiated a 3-5 year plan of at least 10% annual EPS growth.

The airline reported a $6.25 EPS for 2023 and again guided to a 2024 EPS of $6 to $7. In essence, Delta Air Lines has now guided to an EPS of around $7 for 2024, 2025, and 2026.

The consensus analyst estimates expected Delta to guide up to a $7.28 EPS for 2026 and move up to the $8+ range in 2027, but the initial 2026 guidance is only a midpoint of $7. The stock would have considerable upside with a market confident in the airline hitting an $8 EPS in 2027.

Regardless of the struggle to boost EPS above a ceiling, Delta Air Lines continues to report an impressive improvement in the balance sheet. On the Q4 '25 earnings release, CFO Daniel Janki made the following statement:

After reinvesting $4.3 billion in the business in 2025, Delta generated $4.6 billion of free cash flow. Strong cash generation enabled continued debt paydown, improving gross leverage to 2.4x. We closed the year with adjusted net debt of approximately $14 billion and unencumbered assets of $35 billion, positioning Delta with the strongest balance sheet and the highest credit quality in our history.

Investors are increasingly realizing that an airline having debt isn't an issue. What really matters is the net debt levels compared to the PP&E. In this case, Delta Air Lines has $39.7 billion in net PP&E with $35 billion in unencumbered assets, allowing the airline to easily borrow against those assets in any future downturn.

Delta produced $4.6 billion in free cash flow last year, but the airline also generated the operating cash flows to invest $4.3 billion in new equipment. The airline will boost investment in 2026 to $5.5 billion, mainly in new aircraft like the recent deal to acquire 30 The Boeing Company (BA) 787 Dreamliners.

Whether or not the airline grows annual profits by 10%, Delta Air Lines will generate the free cash flow to return to shareholders. The company currently has a 1.1% dividend yield amounting to ~$500 million in annual payouts. The stock only has a $46 billion market cap, so a few billion in share buybacks could help boost EPS via a reduction in outstanding shares.

The stock is relatively cheap at just 10x EPS targets. The airline needs to build a real path to actually reporting a $7 EPS and confidence in topping $8 in the future years, similar to consensus estimates.

The biggest risk to the investment story is disruptions in the business, similar to last year with the crashes. In addition, President Trump pushing for a 10% interest rate cap on credit cards could impact loyalty revenues, with the airline collecting $8.2 billion in remuneration from American Express Company (AXP) in 2025.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Delta Air Lines is relatively cheap here compared to the earnings potential. The airline has to execute on pushing the earnings picture to the next level up with 10% annual growth in order to reward shareholders.

data.title: Franklin International Growth Equity ADR SMA Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: International equities collectively moved higher over the third quarter of 2025, though lagging the gains seen in other regions. Despite some volatility during the quarter, particularly following US economic data releases, investors welcomed easing trade tensions and optimism surrounding artificial intelligence (AI) advancements.
  • Contributors: Stock selection in the health care sector bolstered returns relative to the MSCI EAFE Index-NR.
  • Detractors: Stock selection in the materials and industrials sectors held back relative returns.
  • Outlook: We believe we are entering a more constructive economic and market environment, with potential inflection points including a more favorable interest-rate environment and improved market breadth.

Performance Review

  • In the materials sector, Sika, a Swiss specialty chemicals company, held back relative performance due to challenging market conditions and project delays. The company struggled with US tariff uncertainties and subdued Chinese demand, which pushed back its projected growth targets. Despite positive signals in Europe, weakness in the US market—with new construction activity constrained by macroeconomic uncertainty and higher interest rates—as well as currency movements provided additional headwinds.
  • Among industrials, Denmark-based global transport and logistics company DSV (DSDVF) detracted from relative performance. The company’s road and logistics divisions faced weaker-than-expected results, while the contribution to earnings from its recent Schenker acquisition also fell short. The results were largely driven by the adjustment of global trade flows following the implementation of US tariffs as well as persistent weak economic conditions in Europe, though lately these have shown signs of bottoming.
  • In contrast, Dutch biotechnology company argenx (ARGX) contributed to performance after the sales of its autoimmune drug Vyvgart came in well ahead of consensus expectations, with the drug’s strong momentum in the treatment of certain autoimmune disorders, stabilizing net pricing and rising international adoption all boosting investor confidence. The company’s operating margin and earnings also exceeded forecasts, and its sustained profitability along with a healthy cash balance reinforced positive sentiment about its long-term growth outlook.

Outlook

  • We believe we are entering a more constructive economic and market environment, as headwinds that have affected our strategy look set to diminish. These potential inflection points include a more favorable interest-rate environment and improved market breadth—with mid- and small-cap equities now trading at meaningful discounts to large caps, an easing of monetary policy and normalization of financing conditions could see these valuations narrow.
  • Looking ahead, US trade policy seems likely to drive further volatility in coming months, as new country and industry tariffs come into effect. Our view here is unchanged, in that we expect events to remain fluid and uncertain until the back and forth between trading partners becomes more settled. The extent of tariff-related concerns about slowing economic growth or recession, higher inflation and supply chain disruptions remains highly uncertain. However, we are mindful that economic data is backward-looking and while inventory management has afforded many companies the capacity to mitigate tariff impacts, that flexibility will likely be only temporary. Elsewhere, the US government shutdown and political turmoil in France, both of which have the potential to increase market volatility, are situations we continue to monitor closely.

Benchmark(s)

Benchmark =MSCI EAFE Index-NR

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: BrandywineGLOBAL - Global Opportunities Bond Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: The global fixed income market continued its upward movement over the third quarter, adding to its year-to-date gains. Developed market yields were mixed, with rates falling in the U.S., while generally rising elsewhere. Trade negotiations, questions tied to future monetary policy, and geopolitical events were some of the factors impacting the market. Turning to monetary policy, as widely telegraphed, the Federal Reserve (Fed) cut rates 25 basis points (bps) at its meeting in September, the first reduction of the year. The European Central Bank (ECB) and Bank of Japan (BoJ) held rates steady over the quarter, while the Bank of England (BOE) cut rates in August and then held steady in September. The 10-year US Treasury yield fell, ending the quarter eight basis points (bps) lower at 4.16%. For the quarter, the Bloomberg Global Aggregate Bond Index gained 0.60%, raising its year-to-date return to 7.91%.
  • Contributors: The Fund’s rates positioning added to performance over the quarter.
  • Detractors: Given the U.S. dollar’s strength, the portfolio’s non-U.S. currency positioning detracted from returns.
  • Outlook: We think the “soft landing” camp has been the clear winner, but fading labor momentum and policy cross‑currents argue for more caution moving forward.

Performance Review

  • The largest contributor to performance was an overweight in Mexican duration, supported by elevated real yields and softer domestic growth.
  • U.S. agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) contributed to results, as demand strengthened.
  • U.S. Treasuries were additive for returns; rates declined as labor market data softened.
  • An overweight in U.K. gilts detracted from performance amid increased concerns surrounding Chancellor Reeves’s budget proposal and persistent inflation.
  • In currencies, the Japanese yen was the largest detractor on broad U.S. dollar strength. An overweight to the South Korean won also detracted on structural outflows. An overweight to the Brazilian real was a bright spot, aided by a supportive emerging markets backdrop and hawkish rhetoric from Brazil’s central bank.

Outlook

  • Heading into the fourth quarter, we think the “soft landing” camp has been the clear winner, but fading labor momentum and policy cross‑currents argue for more caution moving forward. Global growth has proved more resilient to tariff shocks than feared, helped by slower price pass‑through, healthy private sector balance sheets, and an artiﬁcial intelligence‑driven capital spending boom. Yet U.S. job creation has cooled toward zero, leaving an uneasy gap between GDP and employment.
  • Risks as we enter the fourth quarter include a prolonged U.S. government shutdown that would dent activity and delay key economic data, as well as a Supreme Court ruling on emergency‑based tariffs that could reset the trade regime. Against this backdrop, G10 rate valuations look roughly neutral.
  • Near term, European political uncertainty and Japan’s leadership outcome, tilting toward ﬁscal stimulus, could buoy the U.S. dollar and nudge global long bond yields higher. Medium term, stretched U.S. dollar valuations and deteriorating U.S. ﬁscal dynamics support our medium-term bearish U.S. dollar view.
  • Select emerging local markets look attractive with elevated real yields, attractive foreign exchange valuations, and limited imbalances. Investment-grade credit remains vulnerable given current spread levels.
  • Overall, resilience persists, but its durability now hinges on real income growth and the staying power of AI‑related investment.

Fund Characteristics

Fund

Distribution Frequency

Quarterly

30-Day SEC Yield (Class IS)—With Waiver

5.83%

30-Day SEC Yield (Class IS)—Without Waiver

5.83%

Average annual total returns and fund expenses (%) - as of September 30, 2025

Benchmark(s)

Benchmark =FTSE World Government Bond Index

Performance data quoted represents past performance, which does not guarantee future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the figures shown. Principal value and investment returns will fluctuate, and investors’ shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than the original cost. Performance would have been lower if fees had not been waived in various periods. Total returns assume the reinvestment of all distributions and the deduction of all fund expenses. Returns with sales charge reflect a deduction of the stated maximum sales charge. An investor cannot invest directly in an index, and unmanaged index returns do not reflect any fees, expenses or sales charges. Returns for periods of less than one year are not annualized. All classes of shares may not be available to all investors or through all distribution channels. For current month-end performance, please visit franklintempleton.com.

Gross expenses are the fund’s total annual operating expenses as of the fund’s prospectus available at the time of publication. Net expenses are capped under a contractual agreement, which cannot be terminated prior to 12/31/2026 without Board consent. Actual expenses may be higher and may impact portfolio returns.

Maximum sales charges have been updated as of August 15, 2022, please refer the Fund’s prospectus for more information.

What are the Risks?

All investments involve risks, including possible loss of principal. Fixed income securities involve interest rate, credit, inflation and reinvestment risks, and possible loss of principal. As interest rates rise, the value of ﬁxed income securities falls. Low-rated, high-yield bonds are subject to greater price volatility, illiquidity and possibility of default. International investments are subject to special risks, including currency fluctuations and social, economic and political uncertainties, which could increase volatility. These risks are magniﬁed in emerging markets. The portfolio is non-diversified and may invest in a relatively small number of issuers, which may negatively impact the fund’s performance and result in greater fluctuation in the value of the fund’s shares. Leverage increases the volatility of investment returns and subjects investments to magniﬁed losses and a decline in value. The manager may consider environmental, social and governance (ESG) criteria in the research or investment process; however, ESG considerations may not be a determinative factor in security selection. In addition, the manager may not assess every investment for ESG criteria, and not every ESG factor may be identiﬁed or evaluated. These and other risks are discussed in the fund’s prospectus.

Glossary

A basis point (bp, or bps) is one one-hundredth of one percent (1/100% or 0.01%).

Duration is a measure of the sensitivity of a bond’s price to changes in interest rates.

The G10 is a group of the 11 leading industrial countries: Belgium, Canada, France, Germany, Italy, Japan, the Netherlands, Sweden, Switzerland, the United Kingdom and the United States. They meet on an annual basis to discuss economic, monetary and ﬁnancial matters.

Gilts are bonds that are issued by the British government, and they are generally considered low-risk investments.

Important Information

The information provided is not a complete analysis of every material fact regarding any country, market, industry, security or fund. Because market and economic conditions are subject to change, comments, opinions and analyses are rendered as of the date of this material and may change without notice. A portfolio manager’s assessment of a particular security, investment or strategy is not intended as individual investment advice or a recommendation or solicitation to buy, sell or hold any security or to adopt any investment strategy; it is intended only to provide insight into the fund’s portfolio selection process. Holdings are subject to change.

The FTSE World Government Bond Index (“WGBI”) measures the performance of ﬁxed-rate, local-currency, investment-grade sovereign bonds. The WGBI currently comprises sovereign debt from multiple countries, denominated in a variety of currencies. The WGBI provides a broad benchmark for the global sovereign ﬁxed income market. Source: FTSE.

Important data provider notices and terms available at www.franklintempletondatasources.com. All data is subject to change.

The 30-day SEC yield is calculated using the net income (interest and dividends) per share earned over a trailing 30-day period (annualized), divided by the fund’s share price at the end of that period. It may not equal the fund’s actual income distribution rate, which reflects the fund’s past dividends paid to shareholders.

© Franklin Distributors, LLC. Member FINRA/SIPC.

Brandywine Global Investment Management, LLC and Franklin Distributors, LLC, are Franklin Templeton companies.

Not FDIC Insured | No Bank Guarantee | May Lose Value

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: PVAL: Historical Outperformance May Not Continue
data.description:
The Putnam Focused Large Cap Value ETF (PVAL) is an actively managed ETF which provides investors with exposure to U.S. large cap value stocks. PVAL launched in May 2021, has net assets of $6.9 billion, and a net expense ratio of 0.55%.

PVAL has delivered solid results for investors thus far, and the fund is rated Buy by Seeking Alpha analysts. While the fund's historical performance to date is impressive, it has occurred over a fairly short-time period. I view PVAL's relatively high level of fees as an important headwind. Moreover, I also have a somewhat cautious view on a number of PVAL's largest holdings. For these reasons, I am not convinced that PVAL's historical outperformance will continue.

Expense Ratio Analysis

PVAL has a net expense ratio of 0.55%. Comparably, the iShares Russell 1000 Value ETF (IWD) has an expense ratio of 0.18%. Actively managed ETF peers include the T Rowe Price Value ETF (TVAL) and the J.P. Morgan Active Value ETF (JAVA) which have expense ratios of 0.33% and 0.44% respectively. Moreover, the average expense ratio for equity mutual funds is estimated to be 0.40% which suggests that PVAL's expense ratio is elevated compared to other active funds.

I view high fees as a key negative, as studies have historically shown that active managers typically underperform their benchmarks over long-periods of time. One reason for this is that it is very difficult for actively managed funds to make up the headwind related to fees. For this reason, when considering an active approach, I tend to favor lower cost options. Thus, I view PVAL's relatively high expense ratio as a negative.

Historical Performance Analysis

As shown by the chart below, since inception, PVAL has delivered a total return of 96%. Comparably, over the same time period, the S&P 500 and iShares Russell 1000 Value ETF (IWD) have delivered total returns of 77% and 49% respectively. Given the fact that PVAL has significantly outperformed the S&P 500 and IWD, I believe it is important to also consider realized volatility to get a sense if PVAL has been taking more risk than these peers. For example, a significant overweight to higher beta or more cyclical stocks can drive outperformance relative to traditional broadly diversified exposure. Additionally, a highly concentrated portfolio also has the potential to drive outperformance but can be riskier due to single stock concentration.

The chart below shares that, since inception, PVAL has experienced an average 30-day realized volatility of 14.5%. This level of realized volatility is lower than the same metric for the S&P 500 of 15.8%. IWD has realized a slightly lower volatility of 14.1%. I believe these numbers suggests that PVAL has not taken outsized levels of risk to drive performance. Rather, PVAL's strong performance has been driven by high quality stock selection.

Overall, I view PVAL's historical performance as a positive, but it is important to note that the fund's track record is currently less than five years. Additionally, I believe it is critical to note that studies suggests that persistence generally does not occur with active management. Thus, in my view, the fact that PVAL has delivered strong historical relative outperformance is not a strong indicator of future outperformance. For this reason, I believe it is more important to consider PVAL's current positioning in order to determine a view regarding future outperformance potential.

Holdings Analysis

PVAL is a fairly concentrated fund, as it holds less than 50 stocks. Comparably, IWD holds 870 stocks. Generally speaking, for actively managed funds, I view high levels of concentration as a positive as it means active managers are focused on scaling up their best ideas as opposed to a more diversified exposure which tends to result in exposure more inline with benchmarks. As I recently discussed in my piece MGK: High Concentration Is A Positive, a number of the most successful fundamental investors including Warren Buffett, Bill Ackman, Chris Hohn, and Will Danoff have historically run highly concentrated portfolios. Moreover, studies have shown that just 4% of stocks are responsible for all shareholder wealth creation since 1926. That said, concentration only works if one can select the correct stocks, and thus I believe forming a view on PVAL's top holdings is essential.

PVAL's two largest holdings are Citigroup and Walmart, which account for 4.5% and 3.9% of fund assets respectively. Comparably, these names account for 0.57% and 1.45% of IWD, respectively. Thus, these two positions represent high active share bets for PVAL relative to IWD. Citigroup shares have performed quite well over the past couple years as the company has executed on an aggressive cost-cutting plan and has started divesting non-core businesses in order to improve focus and returns on invested capital. As a result, Citigroup shares are no longer trading at a substantial valuation discount to peers such as Bank of America and Wells Fargo, which have historically been viewed as higher quality in nature. Moreover, the stock has also experienced significant multiple expansion on a standalone basis. I have a somewhat cautious outlook for interest rates and expect heightened curve volatility, which has the potential to be a negative for balance sheet intensive companies such as Citigroup. For these reasons, I am somewhat cautious on the stock and would not want to have a large overweight. I view PVAL's significant overweight to this stock as a negative.

Walmart shares have also experienced very strong performance over the past couple years. However, the stock now trades at a forward P/E ratio of 38x, which represents a significant premium to the broader market and the stock's own recent average historical valuation. As recently as 2024, the stock had traded at a P/E ratio of less than 24x. I view a forward multiple of 38x as stretched for the company as consensus estimates call for low double-digit EPS growth over the next few years, which is roughly inline with the broader market. Moreover, I expect Walmart to continue to face competitive challenges from Amazon and Costco. For these reasons, I am somewhat cautious on the stock and view PVAL's significant overweight to the stock as a negative.

In terms of sector exposure, PVAL is fairly well aligned with IWD. Both PVAL and IWD have significantly less exposure to the tech sector compared to the S&P 500. I remain fairly bullish on this sector night now, and thus view PVAL's tech sector exposure of just 10% as a negative relative to the S&P 500.

Overall, I view PVAL's high degree of stock concentration as a positive, but my cautious view of its top holdings is a negative. Additionally, I think PVAL's relatively small exposure to technology has the potential to result in a significant underperformance relative to the broader market.

Conclusion

PVAL is an actively managed large cap value ETF which has delivered very strong results for investors since inception. PVAL has a relatively high expense ratio compared to passive options and other actively managed value ETFs. The fund's strong performance does not appear to be driven by outsized risk taking, as realized volatility has been lower than the broader market.

PVAL's portfolio is highly concentrated, as it has exposure to less than 50 stocks. While I generally view high levels of concentration as a positive, I am cautious regarding the outlook for two of PVAL's largest holdings: Citigroup and Walmart. Moreover, I view PVAL's low degree of tech exposure negative relative to the broader market. For these reasons, I am somewhat cautious on PVAL and rate the ETF a Hold.

data.title: Vaalco Energy: We Loaded Our Wagon Recently
data.description:
Introduction

I took a position in Vaalco Energy, (EGY) last week. I was actually so enthused about the entry point that I jumped in before I'd completed this current report. Of course I notified members of the Daily Drilling Report when the trade cleared to give them the opportunity to make a similar move. I think the company represents a compelling value at current levels, and in any rational oil market will rerate higher. We have no idea when rationality will return to the oil market, but we think it will be likely be sooner rather than later. We wrote Vaalco up favorably with a buy in March of 2024 at around $4.00, just before it began a nice rally into the low $7's.

The weakness in crude prices took the stock back into the low $3's by the middle of last year, where it has largely remained. Missing Q-3 revenue and EPS forecasts has also put a pall on the stock along with an anemic forecast for Q-4 of $-0.01, and may delay any sort of rally absent a boost from oil prices.

I've discussed in recent articles that I think energy equities will get a reflation bid this year. But, the current thesis for EGY isn't based on that alone, obviously as this idea is dependent upon a strong catalyst for oil landing. This may or may not happen, but we have to invest our money in real time, and we need to understand the internal catalysts for the company. I did some of this the other day before plunking down my money to enter the stock, but now it's time for a deeper dive for the benefit of our readers.

As usual I like to touch base with the analysts that follow the company to establish a reference point for valuation. The company isn't widely covered, but two of the analysts who do follow the stock have it rated as a Strong Buy. Three analysts are forecasting a rally in the stock between $7.25-$10.29 per share, which is certainly enough to pique our interest. But we need details, so let's have a look.

The thesis for EGY

There is no question that Africa will be a prime market for dispatchable energy. The continent is in an energy crisis that only development of its indigenous resources can address. Unlike Europe, India, or Asia, most countries in Africa, particularly sub-Saharan countries, are too poor to import energy. Conversely most of the growth in the global middle class is projected to be in Africa for the next couple of decade, perhaps reaching 1.1 bn people by 2060. As a final driver there is no prosperity without energy security. If there is a more perfect storm in the energy front, I can't think of it.

Against that backdrop we have EGY with a broad footprint in some of the nations where development is underway. That combined with a long history of operating in this theatre establishes a strong case for owning the company.

EGY has been struggling through the impact from loss of production of a suspended drilling campaign in Gabon, and the on-going maintenance work for FPSO for the Baobab field in Cote d'Ivore. Cash flow has been adversely affected leading the company carrying a LT debt balance of $60 mm for the quarter. All of that said, these situations are well on the way to being resolved favorably for the company. Drilling is now underway in Gabon on a multi-well campaign that should be additive to production in late 26 and into 2027. There are reasonable prospects for a cash flow uptick when the company reports Q-4 in March.

Vaalco also has exploration blocks in Gabon, the Niosi Marin and the Guduma Marin, under evaluation. EGY and partners and the operator, BW Energy, have a seismic survey for Niosi is being planned for acquisition in late 2025 or early 2026. The proximity of these blocks to Etame and Dussafu, raises confidence about their future development potential. The company noted a successful outcome for the H2S problem on the Ebouri 4H well. H2S is a poisonous byproduct of petroleum production that crops up occasionally. It is very dangerous, and specialized equipment is needed to treat it. Fortunately this technology is field proven over decades and is not especially expensive to implement.

The Baobab FPSO will back on station in time for new drilling in the field in 2026. The company has also been granted a 10-year extension on the license for CI-40, giving a clear view for production over the 2028-2038 decade.

The company is paying a near 7% yielding dividend that is covered by cash flow, but is nearing a trouble zone with a payout ratio of 91%.

Catalysts for EGY

EGY is now processing seismic for the CI-705 block where, after a farm-in they hold a 70% WI. The blocks adjacency to the Baobab platform gives confidence that will be sustained with drilling that it's connected to the petroleum system and enhances its prospectivity. News of a successful test would be very supportive for the stock.

Additional drilling in Egypt and Canada is also slated for 2026,and frac results are being evaluated for the Western Desert of that country. Farther upstream, the company's development plan for Block P in Equitorial Guinea is progressing and approved by EG's government.

Risks

A lot of the thesis for taking a position in EGY lies in the near term production anticipated from the Gabon drilling program. Although the company has put in "the work" in terms of analysis, nothing is guaranteed until you drill it. There is a good measure of confidence that comes from the company's experience in this area. I won't be losing sleep waiting for the DST report.

A further decline in oil prices-which isn't an unrealistic potential from increased Venezuelan production could degrade the company's financial outlook.

Since this initial report was written in late December, the U.S. has taken control of Venezuela's oil exports, and expects to control the entire industry for the foreseeable future. This may create opportunities in that country, but we think production ramps will be over a matter of years as a number of obstacles remain. Over the short run we don't see a big impact from increased Venezuelan production on oil prices. If this turns out to be wrong EGY could see further headwinds.

Your takeaway

Regardless of near term risks, EGY trades at a very cheap EV/EBITDA multiple and flowing barrel numbers, 2.55X and using the upper range of EGY's production forecast for 2025, $20K per bbl respectively. In my view most of the risk has been taken out of the stock at current prices, subject to the caveats noted above.

I've chased this stock for a couple of years and missed it on key down cycles. To prevent that from happening again, I had opened a good til cleared order for a break below $3.50. In late December that situation prevailed, and "Bam," I was in. I was tickled pink to get in in the mid-$3's, and the action in the stock has reinforced that view. After completing this report, I think I made a good call.

Something worth mentioning is the experience of the Vaalco Management team. George Maxwell, CEO leads an experienced team with a singular focus on West Africa. Management matters, and I think EGY ticks that box.

data.title: Aptiv: Positive On Non-Automotive Diversification And Good Risk Management
data.description:
I've stuck with a “Buy” rating for Aptiv PLC (APTV).

I like its efforts to get into new markets and the way it has protected its downside.

My earlier August 18, 2025, update highlighted the upsides pertaining to its “Electrical Distribution Systems/EDS” division spin-off. This corporate action is on track to be concluded by the end of 1Q26, as per its “Investor Day” remarks.

Further Penetration Into Non-Auto Verticals

At the “Consumer Electronics Show/CES” last week, APTV shared that it is “leveraging our leading technologies” in “other mission-critical applications from robotics to aerospace and beyond.”

The above-mentioned illustrates the firm's intention of broadening its presence beyond automotive-focused operations.

APTV stated its 2030 goal of having “40% of our revenues” being “nonautomotive related” at UBS Group's (UBS) “Global Industrials and Transportation Conference” in the prior month. The current figure is “24%” based on its “Investor Day” disclosures.

I'm anticipating that the group will realize rising top line and profitability enhancement for the future. This assumes an increase in its financial contribution from fast-growing and high-margin non-auto businesses over time.

APTV's internal analysis (source: “Investor Day”) points to a projected FY25-28 “revenue (outside of automotive) growth of approximately 8% to 10%.” That is supported by its bullish expectations about the expansion potential in “other industrial end markets.” According to S&P Capital IQ, the sell-side's FY2025-2028 company-wide turnover CAGR estimate is a more modest +5.2%.

Also, management's UBS event comments suggested that there's “higher margin flow-through on the non-auto side.” One example is the “Wind River” unit serving the “Aerospace and Defense/A&D” and “Telecommunications/Telco” end-markets. Its mid-20s EBITDA-to-revenue ratio is far better than the group-level metric (pre-spin including EDS) in the mid-teens range.

There are various signs suggesting that APTV is making good progress in venturing outside its auto-related business.

Its early-January media release noted that “a leading commercial vehicle OEM has selected its Gen 6 Advanced Driver Assistance System (ADAS) to equip its future trucks and buses in India.”

APTV defines its core automotive unit as passenger light vehicles, excluding “Commercial Vehicles/CVs.”

As per the “Investor Day” commentary, it recently secured $0.15 billion of “new business awards” for the “Energy Storage” vertical in 3Q25.

Broker RBC reported on its CES participation in a Jan. 6 research note (inaccessible in public) titled “Aptiv At CES 2026.” The company mentioned at the show that it's seeking opportunities in “air mobility (drones)” and “government/space” over the “longer term.”

My take is that APTV's actual '25-28 annualized revenue growth will be +7%, or superior to the consensus's +5.2% forecast. I also see its EBITDA-to-sales (post-spin excluding EDS) widening from the current 19% to the low-20% range by end-FY28. I've considered the superior financial profile of its non-automotive segments in making this assessment.

APTV Has Managed Its Risks Well

Cost surges and financial distress are things I would be concerned about for any potential investment candidate. The firm has done a good job in these critical areas.

Ratings agency Fitch's Dec. 19, 2025, article indicated that it “has removed the ratings from Rating Watch Negative, and assigned a Stable Outlook” to APTV.

The company's “debt-to-EBITDA” or “gross leverage” metric hit a three-year peak of 3.04 times as of end-3Q24.

But APTV has subsequently brought down this credit ratio to a more comfortable 2.40x by Sept. 30, 2025. It guided at the 3Q25 briefing that “we expect to continue these (debt pay-down) efforts” backed by “the strength of our operating model.”

The lower end of the targeted “debt-to-EBITDA” is 2.0x, which implies room for additional deleveraging.

Separately, APTV is protected against memory price increases to a considerable extent thanks to its self-help measures.

Micron (MU) “Automotive And Embedded Business Unit” recorded a +14ppts improvement in “Gross Profit Margin/GPM” in 1QFY26 (YE Aug 2026) due to “higher pricing.” That would be worrying for most automotive suppliers, but less so for APTV.

At UBS's forum, the firm drew attention to its early-2025 “investment of almost $0.25 billion” in “semis, including memory.” It emphasized that “we're confident that we're in very good shape (in terms of memory inventory buffer) as it relates to the balance of this year through 2026 and into 2027.”

Barclays (BCS) issued a report (not publicly available) named “Hopes Of Improved Growth” on October 31, 2025. BCS's channel checks showed that APTV boasted “3 months of inventory” as compared to “2-3 weeks” for the “typical supplier.” The difference-maker was the former's pre-emptive semiconductor stockpiling.

You Need To Think About These Worst-Case Scenarios

APTV's future financial performance might fall short of expectations, if its non-auto diversification efforts are unsuccessful. That can happen because of stiff competition from incumbents or a failure to understand its new clients' needs.

Elevated memory prices for a prolonged period of time could hurt the firm's margins, as its low-cost inventories get exhausted in due course.

The Final Word

I still have a favorable view of this stock.

I'm maintaining the “10.2x” EBITDA target pegged to the historical mean from my August '25 piece.

APTV's EV/EBITDA has a +32% (10.2/7.7) re-rating potential, considering its 7.7x multiple now.

Its business mix optimization and excellent risk control are the positive catalysts.

data.title: Western Asset Total Return Unconstrained Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: During the third quarter, volatility in both equity and Treasury markets remained relatively subdued. Risk assets performed well, with the S&P 500 Index reaching new highs and credit spreads tightening. US job growth weakened in August, with nonfarm payrolls rising by 22,000 jobs—lower than July’s upwardly revised figure of 79,000 (from 73,000). The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) lowered its policy rate by 25 bps at its September meeting, setting the target range for the federal funds rate at 4.00% to 4.25%.
  • Contributors: The portfolio’s exposure to investment grade corporate bonds contributed to performance.
  • Detractors: The portfolio’s exposure to non-U.S. developed market (DM) positions was a headwind for returns.
  • Outlook: U.S. financial conditions are largely supportive, which, alongside fiscal loosening and solid household balance sheets, points toward a positive foundation for growth. Headwinds remain, including doubts over the state of the U.S. labor market, core inflation lingering close to 3% and the potential lagged impact of tariffs. This backdrop should keep the Federal Reserve on a rate-cutting path, albeit with a cautious view of the inflation outlook.

Performance Review

  • The portfolio’s exposure to investment grade corporate bonds contributed to performance.
  • Duration positioning had a positive impact on performance as yields fell.
  • Agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) positioning had a positive impact on performance as spreads tightened.
  • The portfolio’s yield-curve positioning had a positive impact on performance as yields fell.
  • The portfolio’s non-US DM positions had a negative impact on performance as gilt yields rose.

Outlook

  • Western Asset’s outlook remains constructive, though we recognize that growth momentum is tempered by uncertainty in areas such as inflation, central bank policy, geopolitics and fiscal sustainability.
  • Fiscal vulnerabilities and persistent inflation may see the yield curve steepen further. European growth is expected to benefit from German and E.U. fiscal support with the European Central Bank judging the eurozone economy to be “in a good place.”
  • We continue to focus on disciplined, fundamental, relative value investing, seeking pockets of opportunity in fixed income while remaining mindful of technical and macroeconomic risks.

Benchmark(s)

Benchmark 1 = ICE BofA 3-Month U.S. Treasury Bill

Benchmark 2 = Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Index

Benchmark 3 = ICE BofA US Dollar 3-Month Deposit Offered Rate Constant Maturity

Performance data quoted represents past performance, which does not guarantee future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the figures shown. Principal value and investment returns will fluctuate, and investors’ shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than the original cost. Performance would have been lower if fees had not been waived in various periods. Total returns assume the reinvestment of all distributions and the deduction of all fund expenses. Returns with sales charge reflect a deduction of the stated maximum sales charge. An investor cannot invest directly in an index, and unmanaged index returns do not reflect any fees, expenses or sales charges. Returns for periods of less than one year are not annualized. All classes of shares may not be available to all investors or through all distribution channels. For current month-end performance, please visit franklintempleton.com.

Gross expenses are the fund’s total annual operating expenses as of the fund’s prospectus available at the time of publication. Actual expenses may be higher and may impact portfolio returns. Net expenses reflect contractual fee waivers, expense caps and/or reimbursements, which cannot be terminated prior to 12/31/2026 without Board consent. Additional amounts may be voluntarily waived and/or reimbursed and may be modified or discontinued at any time without notice.

Maximum sales charges have been updated as of August 15, 2022, please refer the Fund’s prospectus for more information.

What are the Risks?

All investments involve risks, including possible loss of principal. Fixed income securities involve interest rate, credit, inflation and reinvestment risks, and possible loss of principal. As interest rates rise, the value of fixed income securities falls. Asset-backed, mortgage-backed or mortgage-related securities are subject to prepayment and extension risks. Low-rated, high-yield bonds are subject to greater price volatility, illiquidity and possibility of default. International investments are subject to special risks, including currency fluctuations and social, economic and political uncertainties, which could increase volatility. These risks are magnified in emerging markets. Derivative instruments can be illiquid, may disproportionately increase losses, and have a potentially large impact on performance. Leverage increases the volatility of investment returns and subjects investments to magnified losses and a decline in value. These and other risks are discussed in the fund’s prospectus.

Glossary

Duration is a measure of the sensitivity of a bond’s price to changes in interest rates.

The federal funds rate is the interest rate at which a depository institution lends funds maintained at the Federal Reserve to another depository institution overnight. Also known as the “fed funds rate”.

Investment grade is a rating that indicates that a municipal or corporate bond has a relatively low risk of default. BBB is medium credit quality rating.

The S&P 500 Index features 500 leading U.S. publicly traded companies, with a primary emphasis on market capitalization. Source: © S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC. All rights reserved. The yield curve shows the relationship between yields and maturity dates for a similar class of bonds.

Important Information

The information provided is not a complete analysis of every material fact regarding any country, market, industry, security or fund. Because market and economic conditions are subject to change, comments, opinions and analyses are rendered as of the date of this material and may change without notice. A portfolio manager’s assessment of a particular security, investment or strategy is not intended as individual investment advice or a recommendation or solicitation to buy, sell or hold any security or to adopt any investment strategy; it is intended only to provide insight into the fund’s portfolio selection process. Holdings are subject to change.

The ICE BofA US Dollar 3-Month Deposit Offered Rate Constant Maturity Index tracks the performance of a synthetic asset paying Libor to a stated maturity. The index is based on the assumed purchase at par of a synthetic instrument having exactly its stated maturity and with a coupon equal to that day’s 1xing rate. That issue is assumed to be sold the following business day (priced at a yield equal to the current day fixing rate) and rolled into a new instrument. Source: The index data referenced herein is the property of Intercontinental Exchange (“ICE”) and/or its licensors and has been licensed for use by Franklin Templeton. ICE and its licensors accept no liability in connection with this use. The Bloomberg US Aggregate Index is comprised of investment-grade, U.S. dollar-denominated government, corporate, and mortgage- and asset-backed issues having at least one year to maturity. Source: Bloomberg Indices. The ICE BofA 3-Month U.S. Treasury Bill Index is an unmanaged index that comprises a single U.S. Treasury issue with approximately three months to final maturity, purchased at the beginning of each month and held for one full month. Source: The index data referenced herein is the property of Intercontinental Exchange (“ICE”) and/or its licensors and has been licensed for use by Franklin Templeton. ICE and its licensors accept no liability in connection with this use.

Important data provider notices and terms available at www.franklintempletondatasources.com. All data is subject to change.

The 30-day SEC yield is calculated using the net income (interest and dividends) per share earned over a trailing 30-day period (annualized), divided by the fund’s share price at the end of that period. It may not equal the fund’s actual income distribution rate, which reflects the fund’s past dividends paid to shareholders.

© Franklin Distributors, LLC. Member FINRA/SIPC.

Western Asset Management Company, LLC, and Franklin Distributors, LLC, are Franklin Templeton companies.

Before investing, carefully consider a fund’s investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses. You can find this and other information in each prospectus, or summary prospectus, if available, at www.franklintempleton.com. Please read it carefully.

Original Post

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
